sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 60-year-old right-handed male, presented with a 2-month history of numbness and tingling in both hands, which began after a car accident in December where he broke both wrists.
He has been wearing a splint on both wrists, with the right wrist being worse.
He also reports mild stiffness in his neck, a contusion to the head, and a history of prediabetes, which he controlled with diet changes.
He is currently taking Coreg for hypertension and Lexapro for anxiety.
He denies any history of thyroid issues.
He has been experiencing fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, always feeling hungry, nail bed changes, dryness, itching, gout, and muscle pain.
He has been having symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome, with tingling and numbness, mainly at night, and occasional pain and throbbing.
He has been experiencing a decrease in sensation in his fingers, particularly in the third and fourth fingers of the right hand and the fourth and fifth fingers of the left hand.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of carpal tunnel syndrome, prediabetes, hypertension, anxiety, gout, and muscle pain.
He has a history of thyroid issues was denied.

Review of systems: The patient has fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, always feeling hungry, nail bed changes, dryness, itching, gout, and muscle pain.
The remainder of the review of systems is negative.
Current medications: I prescribed Coreg for hypertension and Lexapro for anxiety.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The physical exam revealed normal sensation in the right hand, except for diminished pinprick sensation in the third and fourth fingers.
The left hand showed decreased sensation in the fourth and fifth fingers.
Reflexes were 1+ and symmetric, with 2+ knees and downgoing toes bilaterally.
The Tinel sign was positive at both wrists and elbows.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with symptoms consistent with bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, potentially exacerbated by his past trauma to the wrists.
Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; suspected cubital tunnel syndrome; hypertension; anxiety; gout; muscle pain; prediabetes; contusion to the head; mild neck stiffness.
Plan:
I prescribed an EMG test to determine the exact location of the nerve irritation.
I instructed the patient to continue wearing his splints and to return for a follow-up visit after the tests are complete.
If symptoms persist, we will consider a referral to our hand clinic for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery or to an orthopedist for the same treatment.
Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up visit after the EMG tests are complete.
